speaker: interviewer
text: We were talking about your life in Australia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you dream or think about your experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: To be honest with you, I don't. I'm only thinking about it now lately because I did not even think-- thought that I will remember as much as I do because the years and years we never spoke about it. Only lately, when the children, when the grandchildren actually start nagging us. Funny enough, Gilla and Gerry never ask. I don't know why they did not ask, or talk--

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you not speak to them about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because-- I don't know why we did not speak to them. I-- I-- Smilik thinks that he feels guilty because he survived the war, that his-- his mother perished and why-- why is her alive. I-- I wouldn't think-- I wouldn't think in these lines. I-- I-- I-- I think we were very lucky because-- because being sent there to Siberia saved our life. And I don't think that it's just the whole-- the whole survival, our survival is a miracle because what-- what we went though is all that. Thank God we didn't went through the camps like lots of our friends and families went through it.

speaker: interviewer
text: But we had, in-- in a way, somebody asked me that. They-- want can you talk? You did not went through the Holocaust. But-- in-- in our way, it was also a Holocaust. All right, we did not-- our life was not in danger. But lots of people like when we were sent out to Siberia, lots of people were not as lucky as we were because they were sent out to the taigas.

speaker: interviewee
text: If my father would not had a profession, we would not be most be alive today, because they only left the people, the doctors, the-- the engineers. The-- the people with the profession, they were left in that camp in Asino. The other people were sent out in the taigas, and they were frozen to death from hunger, from cold. So--

speaker: interviewee
text: This-- this was the big forests. And they always was 40, 50 degrees below zero. And people just could not survive this, you know, if they were not acclimatized to that sort of climate. And I-- I-- I-- I wouldn't know how many thousands and thousands and thousands of people perished there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in a way, we were lucky that we managed to be where we were. And we survived the war, although we-- we-- we went though hunger. But we were not empiricals, empirically in danger, as the people were in the camps. But everything is-- has to say-- what can I say?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I think back, I-- I can't believe it. I still remember-- now, it coming back to me-- there was a certain grass which was growing in the fields in Uzbekistan, which was edible. And we were running out to collect that grass. And Mother used to chop it. And we used to put it into soup to make thicker the soup, which we used the broth back from the kitchen when I used to stay for hours and hours in a queue to bring it back. I mean, things like that, it's just-- [SIGHS] there was times which really the-- well, all right. But then they said, OK, but you were not in danger with your life. But lots of people died because they did not have the food. They were not killed. But it still-- it was, in a way, it was-- I wasn't-- I mean, so--

speaker: interviewer
text: For a moment, just going back to your schooling in-- in Russia, was it-- were you indoctrinated with communism at--

speaker: interviewee
text: In a certain time, yes, they wanted us to become like-- like here is the Scouts. They wanted us be-- it was called Komsomol. And I remember they asked me, would I join the Komsomol? And I said, I had an excuse that I'm not a Russian citizen, that I'm Polish, and I don't have to. That was my excuse that I didn't do it. But my father wouldn't let me.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sometimes, I did. But then I decided-- he explained it to me. So I listened to him. I always listened to him. And so I didn't join. There was maybe one other which didn't join. But I didn't. And I felt quite comfortable. I wasn't worried. And that-- they didn't say anything. And I explained, look, I'm not Russian, actually. I run away from home during the war. So I was excused. And I wasn't-- I wasn't forced to do it. Put it this way. I wasn't forced. So I didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you ever question your faith, your religiousness during those years?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, I never was ashamed that I was Jewish. I never-- everybody knew that I'm a Jewess. And I wasn't ashamed of it. And I don't think there was any question anybody-- my-- my Russian friends, no Jews, never bothered to say, look, she's a Jewess or something or other. And I never felt this sort of anti-Semitism in Russia. I never felt it at all, whether we were in Siberia. [INAUDIBLE] in Siberia, we were closed in. But when we living in Leninsk, which we lived freely, nobody made any comments. So, I mean, we did not socialize with the Russian people. Put it this way. My parents socialized only with the Jewish people, whether, you know, the people who were-- who were send away as we were to Siberia, which quite a few families came with us, but also, with the Russian Jewish people, who run away from during the war. We socialized with them, but we never socialized-- not as-- was any necessity-- put it this way-- for us to socialize.

speaker: interviewer
text: And today, as far as religion is concerned, are you as religious as you were before?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, definitely not. I maybe keep-- I keep a Kosher home. And I'm a traditional Jew. We keeping all the holidays. But it's not as much as my upbringing was when I was a young girl. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: Finally, do you have a message for your children, your grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I hope they will be-- they grow up. My grandchild-- I mean, my children are already grown up. And I don't think so I can change their mind and tell them what to do, because first of all, they won't listen. And second of all, I mean, they want their way of life. And I think they're pretty set on that. And-- and I don't criticize them. But my grandchildren, I would like them to remember and think back where-- where from they come. I think they get a pretty well education their own home. So and let's hope one thing. I pray to God that such a war will never come again. They-- they won't have to go through what we went through. And that's abut it, and to keep the family together, which is very, very important for me, that I'm trying to keep them, whether the holiday or Shabbats, whatever I can to keep them together. For me, this is very important. The family is number one. I'm very close to my family in Israel. When my mother was alive that I used to go almost every year to see her, whether on my own or with Smilik. Mind you, it took me 15 years to [INAUDIBLE] the first time because I was frightened to fly. [LAUGHS] Then one day, Smilik brought me a ticket. You're going. And I went at that time with Gerry. He was only 10 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: And ever since I used to go almost every year, every second year because I wanted to see Mom. And my mother passed away in Israel about seven, eight years-- hm, eight years-- eight years-- nine years ago. I been a few times. I love Israel. I feel at home there, maybe because my family lives there and I'm very close to them. We all close. And that's is for me, this is number one, to be close. When that's happen, I'm happy. OK? Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a photo of my parents. There's this funny story about this photo. A few years ago, my friends were in Israel and-- and they came back. It was my birthday. And on my birthday, we went out to a restaurant. And the said to me, I've got something for you, which your sister send it to you. I said, what have you got? And they produced that photo. I burst out crying. I couldn't believe it that's a photo like that survived. What happened, my sister pinched it, actually, from the album of my auntie, who was living also in Israel. And she went and made a photo to all four children. And she sent it for me as a present. And now, of course, I cherish it ever since.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's name, who is here, Fryda [? Rapaport. ?] And my father is Yitzak Wircer. They were engaged. I can't remember what year. But my mother was either 16 or 18 years old. And the engagement was, I think, about five to six years until my father came back with his profession. When he came back from Warsaw, he married her.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you know more or less what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] I don't know. I-- could be in 1922 or '21 or-- or whatever. I can't remember exactly.

speaker: interviewee
text: They would not tell me when. But I'm very happy to have it. And I'm cherish it very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken in place, as I said before, Zaklikow. That was the place we were going on holidays every year. On the left is my auntie, Ester, and her husband Israel. And next to them is my little sister, Carmela. And next to Carmela, I am. And next to me, stand is a friend of my little brothers, Smilik, which is next to him. Then it's my father and my mother on the veranda of a house. Maybe we were living during our holiday in Zaklikow. It was taken, I can't remember what year. But it must have been '37 or '38.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. This is a group of my friends taken in Leninsk. In the middle is my friend, which thanks to her mother, we were managed to get out of Russia before everybody else was able to go back to Poland. And we are all friends from school.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was madly in love with this fellow next to me. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And they-- these were all people--

speaker: interviewer
text: And what-- what uniform are they wearing? What hats?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the hats what Uzbeks were wearing. That was a-- a common-- [SIGHS] thank you. [DRINKS] How do you call it? I forgot the name of that. I can't believe it that I could forget that. It's like a-- [LAUGHS] they were like we wearing, the youths wearing yamakas. They're wearing-- how do call this? I-- I can't remember. Would you remember? Would you know about it? No.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Russia, in Leninsk. Yeah, yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: This photo was taking in a-- one of the kolkhoz. I went once with my girlfriend, with then-- with her mother to a kolkhoz. And I was-- they dressed me up in a-- an Uzbek-- like a dress what the Uzbek women are wearing. And my hair was then done up in little tiny plaits. And we were both sitting on a donkey. And we made that photo, and I loved it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is this-- was the kolkhoz near Leninsk?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, it's around Leninsk. And-- the-- the-- the President of kolkhoz wanted badly me for get married to one of his sons. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. And this I was given in year nine at school in Leninsk. That's like a document being one of the-- the best-- put it this way-- pupil in my class. And I was a-- a candidate for a gold medal, which I never received because I didn't finish the schooling, because we left before time from Russia. And from one side I can see Leninsk-- Lenin. And the other one is Stalin. That's-- I cherish that because it's something which was given to me and I was very proud of.

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- in-- in 1945, I think, in June, 1945. It's only a few months before we left Russia.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. This is for-- this photo was taken in Cracow. And mostly the-- the boys of the kibbutz. And--

speaker: interviewee
text: The [INAUDIBLE] kibbutz was where I was. And there were two-- one said Mr. Baker. It was a-- and then a Captain [? Druker. ?] Captain [? Druker, ?] he was one of the-- of the people-- one of the persons who helped searching Poland for children, which were hidden during the war at Christian homes. And one-- once they-- he run around with my father. And they found-- my father found a little girl. Maybe she was 6 or maybe 10 years old. She survived the war in one of the homes of friends of her parents. And she's actually the only survivor from all the family of ours. And she lives now in America in New York. And--

speaker: interviewee
text: Captain [? Druker ?] is on the left, the third one on the left.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the bottom row. And he really helped a lot, a lot of young children to-- to reunite with their families.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they were all in the kibbutz, yeah, yeah, those.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, this must have been taken in year '46 because '47 I left Poland. So it must have been--

speaker: interviewee
text: --taken 1946. This photo was taken in Wegscheid. There is quite a few people who are not alive anymore. They perished during the War of Independence.

speaker: interviewee
text: I am here in the middle. Can I show it? Here. [POINTS] That's me. And this one was a [? Shalia ?] from Israel who is indoctrinating us about Israel. And some friends, this-- this fellow here perished in the Latrun War, and this one also. They were 18-year-old kids send out to the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they only had maybe few months training in Italy. And when they came to Israel, they send them straight away into the war and they perish in Latrun. Have you heard about Latrun? And they all died there. What--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Here's a-- a photo of my girlfriend, [? Lucia ?] [INAUDIBLE]. And she lives now in America. And her husband, [INAUDIBLE] was one of the people who helped Spielberg's project. And when it was taken-- Schindler's you know? And when I saw them in the end of it when Schindler came to-- when he was-- when he was buried, then Mrs. Schindler came to Israel-- he was there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I just saw in the museum there's a book, Schindler's book, right? And I saw that Schindler himself came to the 25th wedding anniversary. And I was her best, like a-- her bridesmaid when she got married. And I was-- I just met her in Israel. And that just brings memories back. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Good. This is a photo taken-- actually, my father send it. He made it. He took the photo. He send it to us from Israel. This my family. From the left on the top row is my brother, Smilik, who is now the-- the Director of the Jewish Theater in Israel. Then that's myself. That's my husband, Smilik, my sister Carmela, and my mother, Fryda, and my father, Yitzak.

speaker: interviewee
text: Was taken that in July, 1948, just a few months after we have gotten-- actually, actually, he didn't send it from Israel because they were still in Italy. They only left end of the year to Israel, end of '48. So my father, I-- I can see an inscription on the back of the photo that it-- my father send it to me.

speaker: interviewee
text: Just a few months after we got married.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Tell me about this.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's our wedding photo taken in [INAUDIBLE], and on the 10th of February, 1948.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that is me and my husband, Smilik.

speaker: interviewee
text: Look how-- look how he's so slim here. I wish he could turn back like that. [LAUGHS] No stomach, nothing. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my brother, Yosi. I was born in 1951, a month after my daughter, Gilla, [LAUGHS] and with his three children. The top there on-- on the left side is Michael. And then is Daniella. And there's Adam.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they all live in-- in Israel.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. Next to Smilik is my handsome grandson, [LAUGHTER] Simon, who is sticking out his tongue now. [LAUGHTER] Then is my daughter-in-law, Linda; my daughter, Gilla; and my son, Gerry; and my granddaughter, Talia. Is missing one more member is Liza, but she's now in Israel. And she won't be coming back till November. She's deciding to go to the army in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHTER] Smilik, would you like to say something?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am very pleased to be here. And if Simon wouldn't cover me, I would be even seen in the film. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: And otherwise, I'm happy to be here. Gilla?

speaker: interviewee
text: Again, I think it's just wonderful that we can all be together and we can appreciate that we are all together, that the war did not-- that the war really brought our parents together. That was probably the positive, the only positive from the war. And-- and I really think that this testimony is something that will be treasured by all of us.

speaker: interviewee
text: I just want to reiterate what Gilla said, that the importance of having this documented and available, not only for us, but for future generations, is a landmark and-- and impresses me very much. And I'm glad to be here.

speaker: interviewee
text: LINDA (DAUGHTER-IN-LAW): I think it's actually important for-- for the grandchildren because they-- they don't know about--

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] LINDA (DAUGHTER-IN-LAW): --the Holocaust. I mean, even our generation, it's a little bit faded--

speaker: interviewee
text: Do you want to say something? LINDA (DAUGHTER-IN-LAW): --but it's getting more faded.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] LINDA (DAUGHTER-IN-LAW): And this is a way that they'll really know what happened. And it'll-- it'll never be forgotten.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tonya. TONYA (GRANDDAUGHTER): [LAUGHS] I also think it's really wonderful. I've been learning a lot about it at school. And I really didn't know about personal stories that much. I knew about the general view.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I did a project, the [INAUDIBLE] Project at school, which is interviewing my grandmother. And it was really good because I got to learn about the [INAUDIBLE] and give it to the rest of my family, so they could also learn about it too.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm-- I'm happy that I've got grandparents. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: What do like to say? You told something before that which you're going to say something. And I forgot.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, you don't want to say.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about just saying that you love your gran and your grandpa?
